use_case: LLM inference and search augmentation

use_case: LLM inference and search augmentation
insight_text: Double NAT on both CKS and EKS sides complicates network observability if IP tracking is required — Alex flagged as known complexity

use_case: LLM inference and search augmentation
insight_text: Tailscale proxy groups allow multiple pod replicas to distribute traffic — resolves throughput and reliability concerns for CKS<>EKS connectivity

use_case: LLM inference and search augmentation
insight_text: CoreWeave Tailscale operator has custom IP range settings to prevent overlap with CKS internal ranges — same customization recommended for customer EKS operator

use_case: LLM inference and search augmentation
insight_text: EKS and CKS Tailscale operators must use distinct tags (e.g. eks-operator vs cks-operator) to prevent ACL conflicts when combined into one Tailscale access control list

use_case: Training large-scale image generation models

use_case: Training large-scale image generation models

use_case: Training large-scale image generation models

use_case: Training large-scale image generation models

use_case: Training large-scale image generation models

use_case: Training large-scale image generation models

use_case: Training large-scale image generation models

use_case: Protein structure prediction and drug discovery ML workloads

use_case: Protein structure prediction and drug discovery ML workloads

use_case: AV simulation, model training for autonomous driving
insight_text: Object storage bucket creation silently fails without an org policy in place — users hit this during onboarding; SA must walk customers through org policy creation before bucket creation

use_case: AV simulation, model training for autonomous driving
insight_text: Object storage performs better than file storage (POSIX PVCs) for ML training workloads — SA actively recommends it; 'counterintuitive but our object storage performs way better than our file storage'

use_case: AV simulation, model training for autonomous driving
insight_text: Default storage quota for POC is 100 TB for both DFS and object storage; quota increase available on request

use_case: AV simulation, model training for autonomous driving
insight_text: Kubernetes alpha feature flags can be enabled on customer request but are off by default to preserve cluster stability — must be requested before production cluster creation

use_case: AV simulation, model training for autonomous driving
insight_text: Node-to-node east-west bandwidth via BlueField DPUs: 2×200Gbps per server, ~100–200Gbps expected throughput; separate from GPU-to-GPU InfiniBand

use_case: AV simulation, model training for autonomous driving
insight_text: H100 GPU-to-GPU InfiniBand bandwidth: 400Gbps per GPU, ~3.2Tbps per full 8-GPU node for collective operations

use_case: AV simulation, model training for autonomous driving
insight_text: SHARP not enabled by default on InfiniBand fabric — enabled on customer request; most customers run collective ops without SHARP

use_case: AV simulation, model training for autonomous driving
insight_text: RTX Pro 6000 nodes do not support InfiniBand — only H100 nodes use InfiniBand fabric; RTX Pro uses Ethernet only

use_case: AV simulation, model training for autonomous driving

use_case: AV simulation, model training for autonomous driving
insight_text: Workload identity federation: K8s service account can be used as OIDC source for temporary object storage credentials — avoids long-lived access keys

use_case: AV simulation, model training for autonomous driving

use_case: High-frequency trading AI, quantitative research GPU workloads
gpu_types: B200 / B300 BMaaS at US East 15 (Chantilly)
insight_text: 4-tier network design (T0-T3) with nine-plane architecture; each plane is a separate T0→T1 path serving GPU/CPU/storage end devices; applied uniformly across GB200, B200, and VR deployments

use_case: High-frequency trading AI, quantitative research GPU workloads
gpu_types: B200 / B300 BMaaS at US East 15 (Chantilly)
insight_text: Grid = 256 racks of GPUs; 1:1 oversubscription within a grid; 2:1 at T2 (grid-to-grid); ~8:1 from T3 to FPS (fabric border switches); HRT's ~3,888 GPUs fit within a single grid

use_case: High-frequency trading AI, quantitative research GPU workloads
gpu_types: B200 / B300 BMaaS at US East 15 (Chantilly)
insight_text: DPU is the VTEP endpoint; fabric is dumb IPv4/IPv6 underlay; north-south VTEPs between DSR and DPU; east-west DPU-to-DPU; BGP EVPN control plane on route servers hanging off FPS

use_case: High-frequency trading AI, quantitative research GPU workloads
gpu_types: B200 / B300 BMaaS at US East 15 (Chantilly)
insight_text: BMaaS model: CW builds and validates entire fabric with CW IPs, then flips to customer IPs on DPUs; customer gets direct connect into DSR; management layer (BMC, switch management) stays with CW

use_case: High-frequency trading AI, quantitative research GPU workloads
gpu_types: B200 / B300 BMaaS at US East 15 (Chantilly)
insight_text: Front-end east-west bandwidth: 2×200Gbps per server, 200Gbps non-blocking guaranteed; ~50Gbps per GPU for NVL platform; B200/B300 specific specs pending confirmation

use_case: High-frequency trading AI, quantitative research GPU workloads
gpu_types: B200 / B300 BMaaS at US East 15 (Chantilly)
insight_text: Customer observed drops/congestion on B200 with bursty 400Gbps interfaces — CW to confirm B200 front-end bandwidth model

use_case: High-frequency trading AI, quantitative research GPU workloads
gpu_types: B200 / B300 BMaaS at US East 15 (Chantilly)
insight_text: InfiniBand back-end always 1:1, no oversubscription; B200 on InfiniBand; GB200 can be InfiniBand or RoCE

use_case: High-frequency trading AI, quantitative research GPU workloads
gpu_types: B200 / B300 BMaaS at US East 15 (Chantilly)
insight_text: Nvidia Spectrum 4 switches (56 10 devices) used at all tiers T0-T3; Nokia or Juniper routers at DSR; same switch hardware for RoCE and Ethernet

use_case: High-frequency trading AI, quantitative research GPU workloads
gpu_types: B200 / B300 BMaaS at US East 15 (Chantilly)
insight_text: HRT WAN requirements: ~1.6 Tbps ingress planned; no additional storage needed beyond local GPU node storage; data pulled over WAN and cached locally

use_case: High-frequency trading AI, quantitative research GPU workloads
gpu_types: B200 / B300 BMaaS at US East 15 (Chantilly)
insight_text: BMaaS IP scheme: customer asked about restrictions on their own IP addressing — CW to confirm; likely limited only by network size

use_case: Decentralized diffusion model training

use_case: Decentralized diffusion model training
gpu_types: H100 / H200 (sought); RTX Pro 6000 as fallback

use_case: LLM inference for writing assistance platform
insight_text: Flex reservation break-even: if GPU utilization is above ~75%, Flex costs more than a standard RI; below 75% Flex is cheaper — customer's diurnal pattern (peaks to 200 GPUs, dips to 41 on weekends) suggests ~35-40% savings vs always-on RI

use_case: LLM inference for writing assistance platform
insight_text: Node boot time for Flex nodes is ~20 minutes from scratch — CW working to reduce to 5-10 minutes; limits ability to match autoscaling curve precisely; customers must plan for blocky step-function scaling

use_case: LLM inference for writing assistance platform
insight_text: CW does not share spot market revenue when released Flex nodes are resold — simplified model: billing stops when node is released; CW takes the resale risk

use_case: LLM inference for writing assistance platform
insight_text: Flex commitment is pinned to specific GPU type (e.g., H100, B200, RTX Pro); GPU type upgrade clause (e.g., migrate to Blackwell) only available on 3-year reservations, not shorter terms

use_case: LLM inference for writing assistance platform
insight_text: Customer unwilling to commit beyond 1 year due to GPU market volatility and uncertain model size/family needs; CoreWeave currently requiring 3-year terms for standard RIs — blocking deal progression

use_case: LLM inference for writing assistance platform
insight_text: Customer requested ability to ramp down committed GPU quantity within a multi-year term (e.g., from 100 to 75 GPUs after Year 1) due to shift toward managed LLM APIs reducing on-prem inference needs

use_case: LLM inference for writing assistance platform
insight_text: Customer requested billing API or FOCUS-standard cost data export for integration with CloudZero cost management platform — no visibility into billing data parity with AWS is a migration blocker

use_case: LLM inference for writing assistance platform
insight_text: Customer expressed interest in CoreWeave's managed inference offering (not self-managed Kubernetes) as an alternative to Flex — lower commitment, greater hardware flexibility, easier ops footprint

use_case: LLM inference for writing assistance platform
insight_text: Flex guarantees GPU capacity availability (that is what the holding rate pays for); on-demand and spot do NOT guarantee availability — only Flex/RI provide hard capacity guarantee

use_case: LLM inference for writing assistance platform
insight_text: Optimal Flex structure: decompose into RI (baseline) + Flex (above baseline) — baseline RI covers always-on minimum; Flex covers variable burst; exact split determined by utilization math

use_case: Federal AI workloads, partnership for federal market entry
insight_text: CoreWeave has no air-gap or classified solution for DoD/IC markets — all workloads require internet connectivity; FedRAMP High and IL5 supported for connected environments only

use_case: Federal AI workloads, partnership for federal market entry

use_case: Log forwarding / observability
insight_text: Customer runs their own Loki and Thanos cluster and needs log forwarding from CoreWeave's promtail into their centralized Loki. Promtail namespace was accidentally destroyed by FluxCD and needs reinstallation. Multiple promtail deployments can coexist if namespaced. Telecaster (PRODENG-389) identified as the solution — planned for early Q3 (from Jira comment, May 2025). Ticket status: Shipped.
comments: CFR-8 | Component: Observability - Logs | Linked: PRODENG-389 Telemetry Forwarding

account_name: Multiple (Mistral AI, Akasa, PDT Partners, Wayve, MLP)
use_case: Multi-cloud private connectivity during POC and production
insight_text: Multiple customers need Site-to-Site VPN service for private connectivity between CoreWeave and on-prem or other cloud providers (Azure, GCP, Fortinet). Direct Connect has too much lead-time for POC timelines; Tailscale insufficient for enterprise multi-cloud needs. Mistral AI requires compatibility with Azure VPN Gateway, Google Cloud Router, and Fortinet; average ~200Mbps per tunnel. Blocking: customers cannot iterate securely during POC without going over the public internet. NETDP-714 (CW Site-Site IPSEC VPN) in progress.
comments: CFR-29 | Component: Networking - Other | Linked: NETDP-714
dedup_group_key: multiple (mistral ai, akasa, pdt partners, wayve, mlp)|2026-W08|Infra|Networking|Customer Requirements (Blocker)

use_case: LLM development and fine-tuning
insight_text: Cohere asked about SCIM/automated user provisioning for console access via Okta — no SCIM support existed, only manual email invite. Feature requested and now shipped: CLDIAM-192 Console SCIM support.
comments: CFR-9 | Component: UX - Console | Linked: CLDIAM-192 | Resolved Feb 14, 2026

use_case: Large-scale model training on SUNK clusters
insight_text: Nodes repeatedly enter drain state with reason 'scheduler: k8s pod deletion timeout for job' when SLURM tries to cancel a Kubernetes pod-based job and the pod doesn't terminate within KillWait. Docs don't warn that this results in a drained node requiring manual undrain. No automated alerting or self-healing — customers only discover via manual detection or support tickets. Opportunity: automated check + proactive Slack/email notification with undrain instructions. Still alerting and requiring manual undrain as of Feb 1, 2026.
comments: CFR-15 | No component set | Recurring issue refs: FD-19089, FD-18286

use_case: Large-scale training runs on SUNK/SLURM clusters
insight_text: Customers (Poolside and Heron, discussed at onsite meetings) want SLURM jobs to automatically suspend and resume when a GPU node is replaced by CoreWeave's Mission Control, minimizing training downtime during hardware maintenance. Currently jobs are interrupted without the ability to cleanly resume. Related discussion: whether existing preemption of low-priority jobs could address part of this.
comments: CFR-40 | Components: AI Training SUNK, Networking HPC/IB, Observability Dashboards

use_case: Object storage for AI training workloads
insight_text: Heron needs RenameObject S3 API support in CAIOS/LOTA storage platform to enable atomic rename operations. Currently unsupported. Plan was to ship in early Q4 2025 (from Aug 8, 2025 comment). Status: In Progress.

account_name: Multiple (Katana, Poolside, Snipe, Hippocratic AI, Outerbounds, Hologen)
use_case: IaC-driven cluster management
insight_text: Multiple customers (Katana/Nexocode, Poolside, Snipe, Hippocratic AI, Outerbounds, Hologen) request ability to create and manage NodePools via the CKS Terraform provider — currently only possible via K8s manifests. Pain: Terraform -target workaround is non-standard; node pool creation should block until ready (like other TF resources); kubeconfig/access token should be exposed on the TF cluster resource. Customers want node pools managed at the same infrastructure layer as the cluster, not the application layer.
comments: CFR-7 | Component: UX - APIs / TF / IaC | Assigned: Sean Chow
dedup_group_key: multiple (katana, poolside, snipe, hippocratic ai, outerbounds, hologen)|2025-W49|Platform|Console / API / Terraform|Customer Requirements (Enhancement)

use_case: Secure multi-tenant VAST storage with per-node NFS settings
insight_text: Heron needs to segregate cluster nodes by IP address to apply different NFS mount settings (root squash vs no-root squash) for different node types within the same cluster. NFS exports are defined by host IP ranges. Blocking: cannot run a secure environment where root is squashed on most compute nodes while administrator nodes can still mount PVCs without root squash for setting file permissions.
comments: CFR-36 | Customer: Heron | Related to CFR-37 (VAST NFS export options)

use_case: Cluster self-management / node lifecycle
insight_text: Customer wants self-service ability to reboot nodes to apply a specific ncore image (kernel version) defined on the nodepool, without requiring CW intervention or scaling the nodepool down and back up.

use_case: Humanoid robot training and simulation
insight_text: 1X Technologies needs Docker support on SUNK compute nodes without sudo escalation (comparable to Nebius). Customer has existing development environment using Docker for Slurm jobs. Currently forced to use Pyxis workaround which disrupts their workflow and has taken time away from testing.
comments: CFR-38 | Customer: 1X Technologies | Related: CFR-32, CFR-34

use_case: Secure VAST NFS storage configuration
insight_text: Heron needs an API to set NFS export options (root squash, async) on a per-PVC basis via VAST storage. Currently no customer-facing control; requires manual CW storage team intervention. Blocking: customer cannot self-serve desired NFS settings.
comments: CFR-37 | Customer: Heron | Component: Storage (VAST) | Related: CFR-36

use_case: RL training pipelines using NVIDIA NeMo RL on SUNK
insight_text: Pyxis `srun --container-name` does not exec into the already-running container within a SUNK batch job — instead lands on the bare compute node. This causes NVIDIA NeMo RL examples to fail without modification on SUNK, while they run unmodified on other cloud Slurm offerings. SA has provided a workaround by modifying sbatch scripts, but customer requirement is for NVIDIA examples to run without modification.
comments: CFR-35 | Reporter: Willy Markuske | NeMo RL compatibility gap vs other Slurm providers

use_case: Image building and RL training on SUNK
insight_text: Customer needs Docker buildx support on SUNK login and compute nodes for building images from Dockerfiles (for use with NVIDIA NeMo RL). Currently blocked from running docker commands as a regular user without sudo escalation; must use external dev environments (devpod or local builds).
comments: CFR-34 | Reporter: Willy Markuske | Related: CFR-32, CFR-38

use_case: SUNK cluster user management
insight_text: With SUP (SUNK User Provisioning), the POSIX username field in the Slurm Attributes Panel in the CKS Console is read-only — users cannot customize it. Username is auto-derived from email signup info. Customer wants ability to specify a custom POSIX username for their Slurm login node account.
comments: CFR-33 | Reporter: Narendra Kumar Vadapalli | Linked: CLDIAM-208

use_case: AI training and evaluation pipelines on SUNK
insight_text: Multiple customers want Docker running from SLURM login and compute nodes without sudo — for streamlined dev experience and for evaluation frameworks built on the Docker API. Currently forced to use enroot/pyxis workarounds. High impact.
comments: CFR-32 | Reporter: Tara Madhyastha | Related: CFR-34, CFR-38

use_case: Secure console access via enterprise IdP
insight_text: Periodic Labs cannot use Google Workspace as an IdP for CoreWeave console access. CoreWeave requires both SAML response and assertion to be signed, but Google Workspace only supports signing one or the other. Customer is resigned to Google Workspace not being an option and looking for a CW-side fix to the signing requirement.
comments: CFR-31 | Customer: Periodic Labs | Component: Security - IAM

use_case: Humanoid robot training and simulation
insight_text: 1X Technologies was confused about LOTA streaming behavior: their pyav client reads the full object then terminates the connection forcibly, causing LOTA not to cache correctly. LOTA responses are not seekable streams (some AWS SDKs simulate streaming but still retrieve full objects). Docs need to clarify that LOTA is not a seekable stream — customers expecting true range-read streaming will encounter unexpected caching behavior.
comments: CFR-30 | Customer: 1X Technologies | Component: Storage - CAIOS

use_case: SUNK cluster configuration
insight_text: Docs for the directoryCache.interval parameter in SUNK login configuration show default as null but do not explain what happens when the value is undefined (when the cache process will run). Customer requested adding a link to the parameter reference page and clarifying the default behavior.
comments: CFR-28 | Reporter: Gayathri Raj Patel | Default ref: sunk/slurm-login/cmd/directory-cache/main.go

use_case: Enterprise CKS cluster with BYOIP networking
insight_text: JPMC needs to bring multiple non-contiguous /20 CIDR ranges for Pod, Service, and LoadBalancer prefixes in their CKS cluster, rather than one contiguous /18. IP address space is scarce in their global network. Currently manually supported via Cilium Multi-Pool for some customers (e.g. Osprey). Likely to become a common enterprise ask as customers with large global networks bring their own IPs.
comments: CFR-12 | Customer: JPMC | Assignee: Brandon Chavis | BYOIP multi-CIDR
